title: O.1 — Bit flags and bit manipulation via std::bitset

content.text: Author’s noteBit manipulation is used a lot in certain programming contexts (e.g. graphics, encryption, compression, optimization), but not as much in general programming.Because of that, this entire chapter is optional reading. Feel free to skip or skim it and come back later.

Bit manipulation is used a lot in certain programming contexts (e.g. graphics, encryption, compression, optimization), but not as much in general programming.Because of that, this entire chapter is optional reading. Feel free to skip or skim it and come back later.

content.code:
int foo { 5 }; // assign foo the value 5 (probably uses 32 bits of storage)
std::cout << foo; // print the value 5

content.text: NomenclatureA bit holding a0value is said to be “false”, “off”, or “not set”.A bit holding a1value is said to be “true”, “on”, or “set”.When a bit is changed from a0to a1or a1to a0, we say it has been “flipped” or “inverted”.

A bit holding a0value is said to be “false”, “off”, or “not set”.A bit holding a1value is said to be “true”, “on”, or “set”.When a bit is changed from a0to a1or a1to a0, we say it has been “flipped” or “inverted”.

As an aside…In computing, aflagis a value that signals when some condition exists in a program. With a bit flag, a value oftruemeans the condition exists.Analogously, in the United States, many mailboxes have small (usually red) physical flags attached to the side. When outgoing mail is waiting to be picked up by the carrier, the flag is raised to signal that there is outgoing mail.

content.code:
#include <bitset> // for std::bitset

std::bitset<8> mybitset {}; // 8 bits in size means room for 8 flags

content.text: Best practiceBit manipulation is one of the few times when you should unambiguously use unsigned integers (orstd::bitset).
Bit manipulation is one of the few times when you should unambiguously use unsigned integers (orstd::bitset).

list: test() allows us to query whether a bit is a 0 or 1.set() allows us to turn a bit on (this will do nothing if the bit is already on).reset() allows us to turn a bit off (this will do nothing if the bit is already off).flip() allows us to flip a bit value from a 0 to a 1 or vice versa.

set() allows us to turn a bit on (this will do nothing if the bit is already on).reset() allows us to turn a bit off (this will do nothing if the bit is already off).flip() allows us to flip a bit value from a 0 to a 1 or vice versa.
reset() allows us to turn a bit off (this will do nothing if the bit is already off).flip() allows us to flip a bit value from a 0 to a 1 or vice versa.
flip() allows us to flip a bit value from a 0 to a 1 or vice versa.

content.code:
All the bits: 00001101
Bit 3 has value: 1
Bit 4 has value: 0

content.text: A reminderWe introduced member functions in lesson5.7 -- Introduction to std::string. With normal functions, we callfunction(object). list: size() returns the number of bits in the bitset.count() returns the number of bits in the bitset that are set totrue.all() returns a Boolean indicating whether all bits are set totrue.any() returns a Boolean indicating whether any bits are set totrue.none() returns a Boolean indicating whether no bits are set totrue.

count() returns the number of bits in the bitset that are set totrue.all() returns a Boolean indicating whether all bits are set totrue.any() returns a Boolean indicating whether any bits are set totrue.none() returns a Boolean indicating whether no bits are set totrue.

all() returns a Boolean indicating whether all bits are set totrue.any() returns a Boolean indicating whether any bits are set totrue.none() returns a Boolean indicating whether no bits are set totrue.
any() returns a Boolean indicating whether any bits are set totrue.none() returns a Boolean indicating whether no bits are set totrue.
none() returns a Boolean indicating whether no bits are set totrue.
